questions.text: I believe that the moral weight of suffering is the consideration that should discipline every exercise of power, whether personal, institutional, or political.
I tend to miss when someone uses a particular anecdote to send a message to someone else in the group.
I find that when I have two options, I almost always choose the one that benefits someone else.
I take pride in the behind-the-scenes work that makes a project succeed.
I tend to feel settled and grounded as my default state.
I feel most confident when I am contributing to a process others are co-directing.
I tend to view others' distress through a lens of objective evaluation rather than personal resonance.
I keep a running list of things I need to remember and review it regularly.
I hold the view that maintaining rational composure during painful events is not a sign of disengagement but of deep respect for the stakes involved.
I find that my emotions are not background music — they are the main event.
I find that I focus on facts and logic rather than reading between the lines.
I find that I can participate in emotionally intense situations without my own system getting activated.
I often find that I am the one defining what success looks like.
I pick up on subtle vocal changes that signal someone is uncomfortable.
I avoid detailed project timelines because they rarely survive contact with reality.
I hold the view that my long-term well-being depends on consistently prioritizing my own needs.
I hold the view that the most important relationship I have is the one with myself.
I work in intense bursts of energy rather than at a steady, measured pace.
